question: do mycorrhizal fungi
title: Do Mycorrhizal Fungi Actually Work? What the Science Says About Nature's Underground Network
metaDescription: Mycorrhizal fungi colonize about 80% of all land plants and move nutrients directly to roots. Scotty explains how this underground exchange works, why synthetic fertilizers destroy it, and how to protect it in your garden.
content:
Do mycorrhizal fungi actually work? Y'all, this is one of those questions where the answer is so emphatically yes that I almost don't know where to start.

Mycorrhizal fungi colonize roughly 80 percent of all land plant species on Earth. That relationship evolved about 400 to 450 million years ago — before most plants had developed significant root systems. The fungi helped plants colonize land by extending their reach for water and nutrients far beyond what roots alone could access. In exchange, plants fed the fungi carbohydrates from photosynthesis. That's a 450-million-year-old trade still running right now in every healthy soil on the planet.

Do they work? They built the terrestrial ecosystem. That's how well they work.

## What Mycorrhizal Fungi Are Actually Doing Underground

Let me give you the real picture of what's happening below the soil surface, because once you understand it you'll never think about fertilizer the same way again.

When a plant puts out roots, mycorrhizal fungi colonize those roots — literally growing into the root tissue and establishing a direct physical connection. The fungal partner then extends networks of incredibly fine threads called hyphae out into the surrounding soil. These hyphae can extend feet away from the root, reaching zones of soil that the root itself never contacts. And because hyphae are so much thinner than roots, they can penetrate pore spaces in the soil that roots can't fit into.

Through those hyphae, the fungi collect phosphorus, nitrogen, sulfur, potassium, copper, and iron from the soil. They bring these nutrients back to the root connection point and deliver them directly to the plant. In exchange, the plant delivers carbohydrates — sugars produced through photosynthesis — to the fungus. The plant feeds the fungus. The fungus feeds the plant. A perfect circular exchange.

Research published in PMC on arbuscular mycorrhizal fungi has found that this symbiosis enhances nutrient uptake, stress tolerance, and soil health through molecular mechanisms that are increasingly well understood. Specific phosphate transporters — proteins called PT4 and PT8 — are expressed in plant roots specifically at the points where mycorrhizal fungi connect, facilitating the nutrient transfer. This is not vague biology. This is documented molecular machinery that evolved over hundreds of millions of years.

## The Numbers Are Staggering

A cubic meter of healthy agricultural soil can contain thousands of feet of fungal hyphae. These networks connect not just one plant to its fungi, but multiple plants to each other — researchers have documented what's sometimes called the Wood Wide Web, where trees and plants in a forest are connected through shared fungal networks that can transfer nutrients and even chemical signals between plants.

A single mycorrhizal fungus can increase the effective nutrient-absorbing surface area of a plant's root system by 100 to 1000 times. That is a massive amplification of the plant's ability to gather what it needs from the soil. No synthetic fertilizer application strategy delivers nutrients with anything approaching that efficiency or specificity.

Mycorrhizal fungi also improve soil structure. As the hyphae grow and die through the soil, they deposit a glycoprotein called glomalin, which acts as a biological glue that binds soil particles into aggregates. Research from Springer Nature has confirmed that mycorrhizal fungi increase aggregate stability and microbial biomass in soil. Those aggregates are what give healthy soil its water-holding capacity, its drainage characteristics, and its tilth. The fungi are literally building the soil structure while they're feeding the plants.

## Why Synthetic Fertilizers Destroy the Relationship

This gets to the heart of why conventional agriculture creates a dependency trap.

When a plant's roots are flooded with synthetic phosphorus and nitrogen — the main components of synthetic fertilizers — the plant no longer needs to invest in feeding its mycorrhizal partners. The deal the plant is making with the fungi is essentially: I'll give you carbohydrates in exchange for nutrients I can't easily get myself. But if the nutrients are just sitting there in excess, the plant stops paying the fungi.

It doesn't. Research has shown that plants under high synthetic fertilizer regimes dramatically reduce their investment in mycorrhizal relationships. The fungi decline. The networks shrink. The underground exchange system that took decades or centuries to build gets dismantled season by season.

And now the plant is on its own. No fungal network to extend its reach. No biological delivery system for micronutrients. No aggregate stability from glomalin. Just synthetic inputs in the soil, and a plant that has lost the partnership that would have served it better over the long run.

Gabe Brown describes this in Dirt to Soil — I keep recommending that book because it explains these relationships better than almost anything else I've read. When he stopped applying synthetic fertilizers and started building the biology back, the mycorrhizal networks returned, and his crops became healthier and more resilient.

## Protecting Mycorrhizal Fungi in Your Garden

Here's what this means practically for what you do in your garden.

Don't till if you can avoid it. Tillage physically shreds mycorrhizal hyphae. Those networks take months to establish and can be destroyed in a single tillage pass. If you're moving toward no-till — which I strongly recommend — you're also protecting the mycorrhizal infrastructure that's already there.

Go easy on synthetic fertilizers, especially phosphorus. High phosphorus availability signals to the plant that it doesn't need its fungal partners, and the partnership declines. If you're building soil with compost and organic matter, the phosphorus in that organic matter becomes available through biological processes at a rate the plant can use without triggering the feedback that suppresses mycorrhizal colonization.

Keep living roots in the ground. Mycorrhizal fungi need a living host to survive. When you have a bare fallow period with no plants in the soil, the fungal networks that depend on plant roots for carbohydrates decline or die off. Cover crops during off-seasons keep the fungi fed and the networks intact.

Be cautious with fungicides. Some products applied to control fungal diseases in crops also affect mycorrhizal fungi. A fungicide doesn't know the difference between a pathogenic fungus and a beneficial symbiotic one. Read the labels and understand the biology before you apply anything.

## The Living Soil Connection

Mycorrhizal fungi are not a supplement you add to soil. They're not a product you can buy that replaces what healthy soil has. They are one component of a living community that includes bacteria, protozoa, nematodes, earthworms, and hundreds of other organisms, all working together in the decay cycle to make nutrients available and maintain soil structure.

You can buy mycorrhizal inoculants — spores in a bag that you add to transplants — and they can be helpful in disturbed soils with depleted biology. But the better approach, the one that actually builds the system long-term, is to create the conditions where the existing native mycorrhizal community can thrive. Organic matter, minimal disturbance, living roots, and no chemical interference.

Albert Howard understood this. He wrote in An Agricultural Testament that the fertility of the soil is maintained by the living organisms within it, and that farming's job is to support those organisms, not to replace them. The mycorrhizal fungi are a central part of that living community.

Do mycorrhizal fungi work? They've been running this planet's agriculture since before there were humans to farm it. Let them do their job.

---

- [Mycorrhizal Fungi Increase Plant Nutrient Uptake, Aggregate Stability and Microbial Biomass](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s13199-024-00994-4)
- [The Mycorrhizal Symbiosis: Research Frontiers in Genomics, Ecology, and Agricultural Application](https://nph.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/nph.19541)
- [Biology, Ecology, and Benefits of Arbuscular Mycorrhizal Fungi in Agricultural Ecosystems — University of Florida IFAS](https://ask.ifas.ufl.edu/publication/PP383)
- [Mechanisms Underlying Beneficial Plant-Fungus Interactions in Mycorrhizal Symbiosis](https://www.nature.com/articles/ncomms1046)

scottyQuotes: Mycorrhizal fungi colonize 80 percent of all land plants on Earth. That relationship evolved 450 million years ago. Do they work? They built the terrestrial ecosystem.
A mycorrhizal network can increase the effective nutrient-absorbing surface area of a plant's roots by 100 to 1000 times. No synthetic fertilizer strategy comes close to that efficiency.

When you dump synthetic fertilizer on the ground, the plant stops paying its fungal partners. The network shrinks. The biology collapses. And now you've got a plant that's completely dependent on inputs you have to keep buying.
